petitioner_background: The petitioner is a dancer and choreographer seeking first preference immigrant classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under INA § 203(b)(1)(A). She bases her claim on her skills and experience as a dancer and choreographer, describing her work as requiring rigorous auditions and rehearsals and collaboration with instructors, choreographers, and fellow dancers. She claims affiliations with Chinese dance organizations including the Chinese Dancers Association and a position as Secretary General of a Chinese folk dance association. The Texas Service Center denied her I-140 petition, and she appealed.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner claimed membership in the Chinese Dancers Association, the Guangdong Provincial Examination District Committee, and a position as 'general manager and legal representative' / 'Secretary General' of a Chinese folk dance association. Supporting evidence included: (1) a translation of a membership certificate with her photo, name, sex, date of birth, and position as Secretary General; (2) a translation of the organization's membership guide stating members are responsible for restoring lost certificates and paying fees; (3) a translated registration certificate titled 'Inheriting and Developing Folk Dance Harmonizing and Unifying Talents'; (4) a translated membership certificate for 'The China Institute of Ministry Nationality Dance'; and (5) a translated 'Constitution' and 'Articles of Association' of the Beijing Chinese Folk Dancers Association. On appeal she pointed to Article 33 of the rules document, claiming it required a Secretary General to 'have the corresponding professional knowledge, experience, and ability, be familiar with the industry situation, and have great impact in the business field of the association.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the evidence insufficient. Neither the registration certificate nor the China Institute of Ministry Nationality Dance membership certificate listed an individual's name, so neither demonstrated Petitioner's membership. She provided no evidence of membership in the Chinese Dancers Association or the Guangdong Provincial Examination District Committee. The translated rules document lacked a corresponding foreign version and a translator's certification as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). Even considering the translation, Article 8 listed membership criteria that did not indicate outstanding achievement was required — it mentioned dance professional education, specialty, engagement in dance activities, and 'certain professional level.' Petitioner's characterization of Article 33 was contradicted by the actual translation, which stated Article 33 pertained to financial management systems, not Secretary General qualifications — an inconsistency the AAO flagged under Matter of Ho. The Beijing Chinese Folk Dancers Association Constitution was submitted without evidence of her membership in that association.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted articles where she was featured or mentioned, published on internet platforms in China. On appeal she argued the platforms were 'well established,' had 'significant monthly traffic,' and were 'recognized as major media outlets in China.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director acknowledged the articles but found Petitioner failed to: (1) submit material establishing circulation statistics for the publishing sources; (2) provide comparative circulation data; or (3) establish the intended audience of the publications. On appeal, Petitioner did not address these concerns or provide evidence to support her claimed circulation statistics or comparative data. The AAO found the record lacked evidence demonstrating the significance of the statistics or evidence that the internet platforms reflected status as major media.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted a document titled with 'Proof of invitation' below the title, discussing details of a square dance competition. She also submitted photographs depicting her seated at a rectangular table with several individuals, including a closeup of a card in front of each individual and a closeup photo with the word 'judge' typewritten below a card and her name typewritten near her image, with 'The Petitioner' handwritten above her typewritten name.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the evidence insufficient. The translated invitation document lacked a translator's certification attesting to completeness, accuracy, and translator competency, as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). The photographs were similarly problematic: the foreign language content was not accompanied by the required translator certification, and the typewritten 'judge' label and name did not appear to be part of the original image, undermining their probative value. The article purportedly discussing the competition she claimed to have judged also lacked a certified translation. These deficiencies precluded reliance on the submitted materials as proof of actual participation as a judge.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted recommendation letters, news articles, and evidence of her work as a director. On appeal she argued she founded a dance form, opened her own training institution, established and expanded 'a dance-focused business,' played a 'pivotal role' in a dancer's association contributing to the 'cultural landscape' through 'strategic vision and business-like contributions,' used an 'innovative approach' integrating dance with live music at a local business district event, made 'impactful contributions' to a multi-day youth dance event where she taught folk and jazz dance technical skills, and served as an 'Industry Expert and Judge' in a 2023 competition. A recommendation letter from Ms. D, a fellow dancer, described Petitioner's 'profound passion for her art, particularly in Kazakh ethnic dance' and discussed collaboration in a musical theater production with ethnic minority dancing and live music. Her professor's letter stated her 'potential contributions extend beyond the stage, as she actively engages with local communities, teaches dance workshops, organizes cultural events, and mentors aspiring dancers.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the evidence insufficient. The Director noted Petitioner provided recommendation letters and articles but no corroborating evidence that her contributions were original or of major significance. On appeal, the AAO emphasized the question was not whether she contributed to her field, but whether contributions were original AND of major significance. Her claim to have founded a dance style lacked corroborating evidence, had not been previously mentioned, and she never claimed to have founded an original dance form. Her 'pivotal role' assertions did not address originality or major significance. The 'innovative' integration of dance with live music lacked evidence of originality or major significance. Articles about the youth dance event discussed the event and sponsors but did not indicate original contributions — one article only mentioned her teaching technical skills. Ms. D's letter spoke highly of Petitioner but did not specify an original contribution or how she significantly impacted the field. The professor's letter identified no specific original contribution. Her role as 'Industry Expert and Judge' in a 2023 competition was both unsubstantiated and irrelevant to originality. The AAO held that establishing a contribution was original is not enough — it must also be of major significance, citing Matter of Chawathe.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence included two certificates of honor for Petitioner's participation in two group dances during dance competitions in 2011 and 2013. The record also contained published materials about a 2018 performance and the artists who created and starred in it, including photographs of the performance and a list of performers comprising the cast (which included Petitioner) along with performer bios including hers.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the submitted documents offered sufficient evidence that Petitioner displayed her work at an artistic exhibition or showcase. The AAO withdrew the Director's adverse determination on this criterion, citing 6 USCIS Policy Manual F.2(B)(1).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner claimed her role as Secretary General of a Chinese folk dance association was leading or critical. In response to an RFE, she submitted documents with the association's name at the heading — one purporting to list the organization's vice chairman, deputy secretary general, and directors, and another purporting to list the association's rules. On appeal, she relied on the same documents plus her membership ID card showing her Secretary General position, and also contended she 'played a leading role in organizing and executing a three-day dance exchange activity' with success 'confirmed by media coverage from major Chinese outlets.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the evidence insufficient. The rules/directors documents contained a stamp with foreign writing on the last page but no copy of the original foreign language version was submitted, and no translator's certification attesting to completeness, accuracy, and competency was provided, violating 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). The membership document, though containing a translator's certification, was also submitted without the original foreign language version. Even if the member ID card complied with the regulation, it contained no information about the duties her role entailed, and position title alone cannot establish the leading or critical nature of a role (citing 6 USCIS Policy Manual F.2(B)(1)). Her role in organizing a three-day dance exchange activity did not satisfy the criterion because, under the plain language of the regulation, the leading or critical role must be within an organization or establishment, not an event.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO withdrew the Director's adverse determination on the exhibition criterion (vii) but dismissed the appeal because Petitioner still failed to meet the required three of ten criteria. She did not satisfy the criteria at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(ii)-(v) and (viii), and the AAO reserved the issue of criterion (i) because even considering that claim would not have brought her to three satisfied criteria. The AAO also reserved the high-salary criterion (ix) as waived because she did not pursue it on appeal. The AAO concluded the Petitioner had not shown sustained national or international acclaim or that she was among the small percentage at the very top of the field, citing Matter of Price for the proposition that even major league-level performance does not automatically meet the extraordinary ability standard. Because Petitioner failed the initial evidentiary threshold, the AAO did not conduct a Kazarian final merits determination.
legal_citations: Matter of Christo's, Inc., 26 I&N Dec. 537 (AAO 2015)
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953 (Assoc. Comm'r 1994)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Multiple procedural deficiencies under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3): translated documents (rules of the folk dance association, judging invitation, photograph annotations, leading role documents) were submitted without the required translator's certification attesting to completeness, accuracy, and translator competency, and/or without copies of the original foreign language versions. The AAO also flagged an inconsistency under Matter of Ho between Petitioner's claimed contents of Article 33 (requirements for Secretary General) and the actual translated text (financial management system), which undermined the credibility of the translated material. Additionally, the photograph purporting to show Petitioner as a judge had typewritten annotations ('judge,' her name) and handwritten text ('The Petitioner') that did not appear to be part of the original image, undermining its probative value.
